entries.intro: I used to help my mother with the garden when I was young. Didn't appreciate it at the time.
details: She'd have me up early doing the boring parts while she did the real work. I complained the whole time.
Funny thing is, I do it the same way now. Her way. Couldn't change it if I tried.
reactions: I wish I'd paid more attention. I only remember the broad strokes.

entries.intro: We used to have a dog that followed my father everywhere, even out to the fields. Smartest animal I ever knew.
details: It could find its way home from anywhere. My father tested it once. Took it clear across the valley and it beat him back.
It slept at the foot of the bed every night for twelve years. Same spot, never moved.
reactions: I've had other dogs since. Good ones. But not like that one.

entries.intro: Things around here used to look different. Smaller, maybe. Or maybe I was just taller in my memory.
details: There was a tree I used to climb that I could barely reach the first branch of. It's not that tall. I was just that small.
Everything felt farther apart too. Walking to the next house over felt like a journey.
reactions: It's strange how places shrink as you grow. The world gets smaller every year.

entries.intro: My grandmother made a stew that I've been trying to recreate my whole life. Never quite right.
details: She never wrote anything down. Just a handful of this, a pinch of that, and somehow it was perfect.
I've gotten close a few times. Close enough that the smell takes me right back to her kitchen.
reactions: I think the real ingredient was being eight years old and hungry after playing outside all day.

statCheck.pass: Old cooks worked by feel because they made the same thing a thousand times. The recipe's in the muscle memory, not the words.
statCheck.fail: I couldn't tell you what she did differently. It just tasted like home.

entries.intro: I've been here long enough to remember when this place looked completely different.
details: Half the buildings weren't here. Just open ground where kids played and old folks sat in the sun.
You could see clear to the tree line from the square. Now there's so much in the way.
reactions: I don't mind the change exactly. I just miss how quiet it was.

statCheck.pass: Places grow toward water and trade routes. It's natural. This settlement filled in the way most do.
statCheck.fail: I don't know why things changed so much. They just did, year by year.

entries.intro: I used to be able to work all day without stopping. Now my back tells me when it's noon.
details: I'd haul timber, dig foundations, carry stones. Didn't think twice about it.
The worst part isn't the pain. It's knowing exactly how long the recovery takes.
reactions: Getting old is just losing arguments with your own body.

entries.intro: There was an old-timer who used to sit by the well every afternoon. Told the best stories.
details: Half of them were probably made up, but he told them so well you didn't care.
He'd give advice too, whether you asked for it or not. Most of it was actually good.
reactions: I catch myself sitting in the same spot sometimes. Haven't got any good stories yet, though.

entries.intro: Nobody does the joinery the old way anymore. I might be the last one who remembers how.
details: It takes longer and the results aren't much better, honestly. But there was a care to it that's gone now.
My apprentice tried it once and said it was a waste of time. He's not wrong. He's just not right either.
reactions: Some things are worth doing the hard way. Even if nobody agrees with me.

statCheck.pass: The old methods worked with the materials we had then. The new ways are faster, but they assume better stock than what we usually get.
statCheck.fail: I can't explain why the old way matters. It just feels right in my hands.
